one_liner: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of painful mouth sores and, more recently, fragile blisters on her chest and back that rupture easily leaving raw erosions.

stage2.hpi_extension: Painful oral erosions began about six weeks ago and have made eating nearly impossible. She has lost 4 kg. Three weeks ago, flaccid clear blisters appeared on her trunk and axillae; the blisters break within hours leaving denuded, weeping skin. Gentle pressure or friction on normal-appearing skin seems to produce new erosions. She denies fever, rash on palms or soles, or recent new medications.
stage2.social_history: Schoolteacher. Non-smoker; rare alcohol. No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: Oral mucosa: multiple painful, shallow erosions on the buccal mucosa, gingiva, and palate without intact bullae. Skin: scattered flaccid bullae and large, irregular, weeping erosions on the chest, back, and axillae with surrounding normal-appearing skin. Gentle lateral pressure on clinically normal perilesional skin induces sloughing of the epidermis (positive Nikolsky sign). Eyes/genitals: no involvement. No target lesions. No lymphadenopathy.

lab_results.result: Punch biopsy of a perilesional skin blister shows intraepidermal (suprabasal) acantholysis with loss of intercellular adhesion between keratinocytes; basal cells remain attached to the basement membrane giving a 'row of tombstones' appearance.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Intraepidermal acantholysis with the tombstone pattern is classic for pemphigus vulgaris and distinguishes it from subepidermal blistering diseases such as bullous pemphigoid.

lab_results.result: Serum indirect immunofluorescence and ELISA: IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein-3 positive (titer strongly elevated); anti-desmoglein-1 also detected. ANA negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Anti-desmoglein-3 antibodies are the pathogenic autoantibodies in pemphigus vulgaris; they disrupt desmosomes and cause acantholysis. Titers correlate with disease activity.

lab_results.result: Swab of erosions: mixed skin flora, no S. aureus or HSV identified. Blood cultures negative. HSV PCR of oral erosions negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Rules out superinfection and HSV stomatitis as the cause of the oral erosions and confirms a primary autoimmune blistering disease.

stage5.final_clue: Direct immunofluorescence on perilesional skin shows intercellular IgG and C3 deposition in a 'fishnet' (reticular) pattern throughout the epidermis. She is started on high-dose systemic corticosteroids with rituximab as a steroid-sparing agent; her oral lesions begin to heal over several weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Pemphigus vulgaris typically presents in middle-aged adults (40-60 years) with painful oral mucosal erosions (the first sign in most patients) followed by flaccid bullae and erosions on the skin, especially the scalp, face, axillae, and trunk. The blisters are fragile and rupture easily, leaving denuded skin. Nikolsky sign is positive — gentle pressure on normal-appearing skin separates the epidermis. Patients can have extensive fluid losses and superinfection, and before the steroid era mortality was very high.
debrief.pathophysiology: Pemphigus vulgaris is a type II hypersensitivity autoimmune disease in which IgG autoantibodies target desmoglein-3 (and, in mucocutaneous disease, desmoglein-1) — transmembrane components of desmosomes. Antibody binding disrupts keratinocyte-keratinocyte adhesion, producing acantholysis and intraepidermal (suprabasal) blisters. Basal keratinocytes remain attached to the basement membrane, giving the 'row of tombstones' appearance. Direct immunofluorescence shows intercellular IgG in a fishnet/reticular pattern. Contrast with bullous pemphigoid, where antibodies target hemidesmosomal proteins (BP180/BP230), producing tense subepidermal blisters with linear DIF.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is high-dose systemic corticosteroids, typically combined with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant such as azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, or rituximab (anti-CD20). Severe disease may require IVIG or plasmapheresis. Wound care, nutrition, and infection prevention are critical. Mortality remains around 5-10%, mostly from infection.
high_yield_pearls: Pemphigus vulgaris = antibodies against desmoglein-3, causing intraepidermal acantholysis and flaccid bullae. Oral erosions are often the first manifestation.
Nikolsky sign positive: lateral pressure on normal-appearing skin causes the epidermis to slough.
Histology shows suprabasal acantholysis with a 'row of tombstones' pattern of basal keratinocytes.
Direct immunofluorescence shows intercellular IgG in a 'fishnet' pattern. Contrast with bullous pemphigoid's linear IgG at the dermoepidermal junction.
Bullous pemphigoid (tense bullae, Nikolsky negative, antibodies against hemidesmosomal BP180/BP230) is the key contrast disease.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Autoimmune blistering skin disorders — Pemphigus vulgaris' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Skin layers and dermatologic terminology' section.
